EDCS-08201396.summary: This is a public decree by the decurions of Sipontum honoring Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely related to a public building or infrastructure project.

EDCS-08201397.translation: To Magia Severina, daughter of Quintus, for the merits of her father Quintus Magius Severus, the town council and the people of Sipontum, by money collected.
EDCS-08201397.summary: This is a public dedication by the town council and people of Sipontum to Magia Severina, likely on behalf of her father.

EDCS-08201398.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Liberalis, slave of the colonists of Sipontum, an oil merchant, who also managed the food supply accounts under the supervision of the prefects for 32 years, had this made in his lifetime for Augurinus, adopted son and slave of the state, his dearly beloved son, a measurer, who lived 23 years, 6 months, and 10 days.
EDCS-08201398.summary: A tomb inscription for Liberalis' adopted son Augurinus, detailing Liberalis' service as an oil merchant and administrator.

EDCS-08201399.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Here the father and mother placed the bones of their son who lived 2 years, 6 months, and 5 days. Publius Vinius Arbula and Rufina Merope, parents, made this for their son Vinius Etruscus.
EDCS-08201399.summary: A tomb inscription for Vinius Etruscus, set up by his parents, who were Publius Vinius Arbula and Rufina Merope.

EDCS-08201400.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To Revidia Tila, who lived 13 years. Sextia Protha made this for her well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-08201400.summary: A tomb inscription for Revidia Tila, who lived 13 years, made by her mother Sextia Protha.

EDCS-08201403.translation: While alive. Quintus Muttienus Phylax, freedman of Quintus, Augustalis, [made this for] his daughter Felicula, freedwoman of Quintus, who lived 12 years and [3?].
EDCS-08201403.summary: A tomb inscription set up by Quintus Muttienus Phylax, a freedman and Augustalis, for his daughter Felicula.

EDCS-08201405.translation: To Georgia, our fellow-worker. Marsua the weighman and Epaphra the land-steward, spouses, made this while alive for their sons Epaphrio, the weighman, Orine, and Delphinus. Also for their daughters Claudia Ariadine and Claudia Helpidi.
EDCS-08201405.summary: A family tomb inscription set up by two couples (Marsua and Epaphra) for their children, indicating familial relationships and occupations.

EDCS-08201407.translation: To Lucius Dastidius Amor. Grania Gemella, his wife, made this for him.
EDCS-08201407.summary: A tomb inscription set up by a wife, Grania Gemella, for her husband Lucius Dastidius Amor.

EDCS-08201408.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. For Euplia, slave of Marcus Ulpius Apolaustus. Dionysius, her partner, and Thallus, her mother, made this for the well-deserving deceased, who lived 33 years.
EDCS-08201408.summary: A tomb inscription for Euplia, a slave, set up by her partner Dionysius and her mother Thallus.

EDCS-08201409.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. For Hermetus, their most sweet and incomparable son. Urbanus, his most unhappy father, made this. He lived 14 years, 5 months, and 8 days.
EDCS-08201409.summary: A tomb inscription for Hermetus, who died at 14 years old, set up by his bereaved father Urbanus.

EDCS-08201410.translation: Maia Optata, freedwoman of the woman, for herself and Nympe in front [of the monument] 3 feet.
EDCS-08201410.summary: A tomb inscription for Maia Optata and Nympe, indicating burial space purchased in front of the monument.

EDCS-08201412.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Pettius Moderatus, who lived 19 years and 5 months. His father, Gaius Pettius November, made this for his most beloved son.
EDCS-08201412.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young boy, Gaius Pettius Moderatus, set up by his father.

EDCS-08201414.translation: Lucius Sestius Salvius, freedman of Lucius, is buried here.
EDCS-08201414.summary: A simple funerary epitaph identifying the deceased and his patron.

EDCS-08201416.translation: For Gaius Titius, freedman of a woman, Como, and Hilara, freedwoman of a woman, Pilia. In front 12 feet, in the field 10 feet.
EDCS-08201416.summary: An epitaph recording the dimensions of a burial plot for two freedpersons.

EDCS-08201417.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Eutiches made this for his well-deserving wife, Symferusa.
EDCS-08201417.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by a husband to his wife.

EDCS-08201427.translation: To Lucius Iulius Faustinianus, a man of most distinguished memory, a former consul, patron of the town council and the people of Larinum, because of his great and constant proofs of love and regard for them.
EDCS-08201427.summary: A public dedication honoring Lucius Iulius Faustinianus, a former consul and patron, by the people of Larinum.

EDCS-08201429.translation: [...] Capito, son of Quintus, of the Clustumina tribe, priest of the deified Titus, patron of the municipium, quaestor, tribune of soldiers of the IV Flavia Felix legion, [ordered] that an amphitheater be built by his will.
EDCS-08201429.summary: An inscription detailing the career and posthumous benefaction (construction of an amphitheater) of Capito, a local official and military officer.

EDCS-08201432.translation: Gaius Iulius Epaenitus, devout, set up this for himself and for Lucius Raius Felix, priest of the Mother of the Gods, a well-deserving man, and for his descendants.
EDCS-08201432.summary: A funerary dedication set up by Gaius Iulius Epaenitus for himself, his freedman and priest Lucius Raius Felix, and their families.

EDCS-08201433.translation: Coelia Tertulla, daughter of Marcus, testamentarily ordered this to be set up for Gaius Paccus Priscus, son of Gaius, of the Cornelia tribe, aedile, duovir, and quinquennial magistrate with jurisdiction, patron of the colony of Venafrum; the site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-08201433.summary: A funerary monument ordered in a will for Gaius Paccus Priscus, a local magistrate and patron of Venafrum, with the site granted by the town council.

EDCS-08201470.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. For Tantilia Faventina, his excellent and most rare wife, and for himself. Publius Luccius Bassus, and for Gavia Titulla, his mother.
EDCS-08201470.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by Publius Luccius Bassus to his wife, mother, and himself.

EDCS-08201484.translation: To Apollo the divine Augustus [...]. Quintus Lutatius Catulus, son of Quintus, of the Claudia tribe. Quintus Lutatius Catulus, son of Publius, of the Claudia tribe.
EDCS-08201484.summary: A dedication to the god Apollo, possibly by two individuals named Quintus Lutatius Catulus.

EDCS-08201485.translation: To Jupiter, the Greatest, Highest, Most Exalted, Marcus Aurelius Augendus, procurator of the Apulum pastures, paid his vow willingly and deservedly for his own safety and that of his son, Marcus Aurelius Montanus, an equestrian of the Roman order. Dedicated on the 6th day before the Nones of May.
EDCS-08201485.summary: This dedication to Jupiter was made by a procurator for his own health and that of his son, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08201491.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication to Julia Maesa, mother of Elagabalus, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08201494.translation: To Lucius Junius Albanus, son of Lucius, of the voting tribe Aniensis, from Hispania Citerior.
EDCS-08201494.summary: This epitaph or dedication identifies Lucius Junius Albanus, a Roman citizen from Hispania Citerior, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08201495.translation: Lucius Anicius, son of Lucius, of the voting tribe Claudia, [---]. Buried here.
EDCS-08201495.summary: This is a fragmentary epitaph for Lucius Anicius, a Roman citizen, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08201496.translation: To the departed spirits. Amandus, a decurion, took care of the burial from his goods for Aurelius Romanus, an equestrian soldier (singulari) of our lord, from the turma of Vitalis in the new barracks, who lived for 43 years and served for 23 years as a duplicarius.
EDCS-08201496.summary: This tombstone commemorates Aurelius Romanus, a cavalryman serving in the Praetorian Guard, and was paid for by his subordinate, Amandus.

EDCS-08201497.translation: Quintus Helvius Bassus, centurion (?). Oppia V[---]
EDCS-08201497.summary: This is a fragmentary epitaph for Quintus Helvius Bassus, possibly a centurion, with the name of another individual, Oppia.

EDCS-08201498.translation: Quintus Nonius, son of Quintus, of the voting tribe Claudia, was buried here [as a soldier] of the VI Legion.
EDCS-08201498.summary: This epitaph records the burial of Quintus Nonius, a soldier of the VI Legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201499.translation: Marcus Oppius, [---], centurion of the VI Legion, first spear (primus pilus), tribune of the II Legion, camp prefect.
EDCS-08201499.summary: This inscription lists the distinguished military career of Marcus Oppius, rising from centurion to camp prefect, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201500.translation: Gaius Valerius Proculus, son of Gaius, of the voting tribe Claudia, from Baeterris, a veteran of the IV Macedonica Legion, is buried here. Gaius Iulius Blandus, his heir according to the will, took care of the construction. Frontage 12 feet, land 10 feet.
EDCS-08201500.summary: This tombstone marks the burial of a veteran of the IV Macedonica Legion, with dimensions of the plot specified, and the heir responsible for its construction.

EDCS-08201504.translation: [---] Achillenius Sabellus, IIIIvir for judging, IIIIvir quinquennalis, [---], pontifex, [---]
EDCS-08201504.summary: This fragmentary inscription lists civic and religious offices held by Achillenius Sabellus, likely from a Roman provincial town.

EDCS-08201506.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Vergilia Prisca, beloved magistra of the Good Goddess. From Lucerinus Herma.
EDCS-08201506.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Vergilia Prisca, a magistra of the Good Goddess, by Lucerinus Herma.

EDCS-08201507.translation: Lucius Vibienus Licinus, son of Lucius, of the Claudia tribe, twice duovir of Sipontum, and four times a magistrate of Luceria. His sister, Vibiena, daughter of Lucius.
EDCS-08201507.summary: This inscription records the civic offices held by Lucius Vibienus Licinus and mentions his sister, Vibiena.

EDCS-08201509.translation: Gaius Obinius Favor, Publius Didiolenus Strato, and the Augustales paved with their own money from the sum of forty (denarii) to the road at Laris as their duty.
EDCS-08201509.summary: This inscription records the paving of a road by the Augustales and two individuals, funded by their own money.

EDCS-08201512.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Naevia Successa made this for her husband, Tito Naevio Donato, an Augustalis, a deserving patron.
EDCS-08201512.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Tito Naevio Donato, an Augustalis, made by his wife, Naevia Successa.

EDCS-08201513.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Ulpia Valeria, wife, and Pilia Valeria, daughter, made this for Aulus Pillius Epictetus, an Augustalis, a deserving patron.
EDCS-08201513.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Aulus Pillius Epictetus, an Augustalis, made by his wife and daughter.

EDCS-08201516.translation: For Gaius Sescennius Bithus, freedman of Gaius, Apollinaris. He lived for 37 years.
EDCS-08201516.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the name and age at death of Gaius Sescennius Bithus Apollinaris.

EDCS-08201518.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Sulpicia Eutichia made this for her husband, Quintus Sulpicius Theophilus Apollinaris, a deserving husband.
EDCS-08201518.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Quintus Sulpicius Theophilus Apollinaris, made by his wife, Sulpicia Eutichia.

EDCS-08201519.translation: [...] public funeral by the decision of Quintus Polit[...], his father.
EDCS-08201519.summary: This fragmentary inscription mentions a public funeral arranged by a father for his son.

EDCS-08201520.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Agre, public slave, from her children to her excellent mother.
EDCS-08201520.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Agre, a public slave, made by her children.

EDCS-08201522.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Felici, public slave, a measurer. [...]
EDCS-08201522.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Felici, a public slave who worked as a measurer.

EDCS-08201524.translation: Marcus Luccius Philocalus, freedman of a woman, a specialist in small cuts.
EDCS-08201524.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Luccius Philocalus, identified by his profession.

EDCS-08201525.translation: Gaius Marius Salvius, freedman of Gaius, a chamberlain, rests here.
EDCS-08201525.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Gaius Marius Salvius, who served as a chamberlain.

EDCS-08201526.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. The household staff made this for Petronia Octavia, well-deserving.
EDCS-08201526.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by the household staff for Petronia Octavia.

EDCS-08201527.translation: Gaius Pontius Erm..., his freedman, a wool-worker, [died]. Prian[...]
EDCS-08201527.summary: This fragmented inscription seems to be a funerary epitaph, possibly recording the names of a freedman and his master, along with an occupation.

EDCS-08201528.translation: For Publius Vedius Carpus, freedman of Publius, a doctor, and for Gaius Lusius Anicetus, son of Gaius. He lived 8 years, 4 months, and 20 days.
EDCS-08201528.summary: This funerary monument honors two individuals, a doctor named Publius Vedius Carpus and a child named Gaius Lusius Anicetus, who died at a young age.

EDCS-08201532.translation: [...] for Alfia [...], his daughter, well-deserving, who lived 16 years and 8 months.
EDCS-08201532.summary: This fragmented funerary inscription records the death of Alfia, a daughter, who lived to be 16 years and 8 months old.

EDCS-08201534.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Rufrius Ferox made this for his deserving wife Annia Modesta.
EDCS-08201534.summary: This funerary dedication was made by Rufrius Ferox for his well-deserving wife, Annia Modesta.

EDCS-08201536.translation: From Numerius Arrius, son of Numerius.
EDCS-08201536.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedicatory or possessive marker, indicating ownership or origin from Numerius Arrius.

EDCS-08201537.translation: Aurelius Crescens made this for his deserving pupil Aurelius Philumenus.
EDCS-08201537.summary: This funerary dedication was made by Aurelius Crescens for his well-deserving pupil, Aurelius Philumenus.

EDCS-08201538.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aurelius Muca made this for his deserving partner Aurelia Antonina, [aged ...5] years and 6 months.
EDCS-08201538.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication by Aurelius Muca to his partner Aurelia Antonina, who lived for a specified number of years and months.

EDCS-08201539.translation: To the departed spirits. To Aurelia Felicia (?), whom Umbrius Iatus lived with for 32 years without complaint; he made this for the well-deserving one.
EDCS-08201539.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a partner for Aurelia Felicia, who died after 32 years of cohabitation.

EDCS-08201540.translation: Babbia Lais, freedwoman of Caius, is buried here, with a front of 12 feet and a depth of 12 feet.
EDCS-08201540.summary: A funerary epitaph specifying the dimensions of a burial plot for Babbia Lais.

EDCS-08201541.translation: To the departed spirits. For Baebius Secundus. Baebia [set this up?]
EDCS-08201541.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Baebius Secundus, likely set up by Baebia.

EDCS-08201542.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Baebia.

EDCS-08201543.translation: To the departed spirits. For Basilius, well-deserving. Epictetus, his wife, [set this up?]
EDCS-08201543.summary: A funerary epitaph for Basilius, set up by his wife Epictetus.

EDCS-08201545.translation: [---] under the direction of Marcus Caesius Postumus [---]
EDCS-08201545.summary: A fragmentary inscription mentioning the direction of Marcus Caesius Postumus.

EDCS-08201546.translation: To the departed spirits. For Camene, who lived 25 years. Itace, her sister, made this for the well-deserving one.
EDCS-08201546.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a sister for Camene, who died at the age of 25.

EDCS-08201547.translation: Capria Felicula, freedwoman of Marcus, for herself and for Marcus Caspilaneus, son of Marcus, here buried.
EDCS-08201547.summary: A funerary epitaph for Capria Felicula, for herself and for Marcus Caspilaneus.

EDCS-08201550.translation: To the departed spirits. For Curiatia Felicitas. Aemilius Felix and Neria Modestina [set this up] for their most dutiful daughter, who lived 18 years, 10 months, and 4 days.
EDCS-08201550.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by parents for their daughter Curiatia Felicitas, who died at the age of 18 years, 10 months, and 4 days.

EDCS-08201551.translation: To the departed spirits. For Decimia [---]. For herself and for Quintus Lutatius Quintus Onesimus. [She/He] arranged for this to be set up, with a front of [---] feet and a depth of [---] feet.
EDCS-08201551.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Decimia and Quintus Lutatius Onesimus, specifying plot dimensions.

EDCS-08201552.translation: To the departed spirits. Calpurnia Galla (?) made this for her incomparable husband Domitius Apollonius, with whom she lived for more or less 40 years, as a well-deserved gift.
EDCS-08201552.summary: A funerary dedication by a wife to her husband.

EDCS-08201553.translation: Quintus Egnatius, son of Marcus, of the Claudia tribe, and Helvia, daughter of Caius, called Posilla, are buried here in accordance with the will, under the supervision of Hil[...].
EDCS-08201553.summary: A burial inscription specifying the burial arrangements made by will.

EDCS-08201555.translation: To the departed spirits. Erucius Hermes and Erucia Aprylla made this for Erucia Successa, their wife and mother, with whom he lived 30 years, as a well-deserved gift.
EDCS-08201555.summary: A funerary dedication by a husband and daughter to their wife and mother.

EDCS-08201557.summary: An incomplete funerary inscription for a girl named Calvia.

EDCS-08201558.translation: To the departed spirits. Gavia Urbana, freedwoman of Caius, lives for herself and for Caius Gavius Onesimus, her patron, freedman of Caius, and for Caius Gavius Acastus, her fellow freedman, his freedman...
EDCS-08201558.summary: A funerary inscription that mentions the deceased, her patron, and a fellow freedman, likely on a tomb intended for multiple people.

EDCS-08201559.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gavia Satyra.
EDCS-08201559.summary: A funerary dedication to Gavia Satyra.

EDCS-08201560.translation: To the departed spirits. The sons made this for their father, Hermadion, as a well-deserved gift.
EDCS-08201560.summary: A funerary dedication by sons to their father.

EDCS-08201561.translation: To Publius Hetereius, freedman of Publius. Hetereia [---]o of the Hetereii [---].
EDCS-08201561.summary: An incomplete inscription that appears to be a funerary dedication, mentioning individuals of the Hetereii family.

EDCS-08201562.translation: For Iulia Apatenis. Iulia Lamyra made this for her mother, well-deserving.
EDCS-08201562.summary: A funerary dedication by a daughter to her mother.

EDCS-08201565.translation: Caius Lucceius Faustus, son of Faustillus, aged 18, is buried here.
EDCS-08201565.summary: A funerary inscription recording the burial of a young man named Caius Lucceius Faustus.

EDCS-08201566.translation: To the departed spirits. Magius Secundus made this for his well-deserving brother, Magius Saturninus.
EDCS-08201566.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Magius Secundus to his brother, Magius Saturninus.

EDCS-08201568.translation: To the departed spirits. Minatia Victrix lived 18 years. Minatius Euaristus made this for his most sweet wife, and also Minatia Felix and Nardis, her most unhappy parents.
EDCS-08201568.summary: A funerary monument erected by Minatius Euaristus and the parents of Minatia Victrix, who died at the age of 18.

EDCS-08201569.translation: His mother and sister placed this for Aulus Muttienus Faustus, freedman of Aulus.
EDCS-08201569.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by a mother and sister for their freedman, Aulus Muttienus Faustus.

EDCS-08201570.translation: To the departed spirits. Iunia Ianuaria made this for her well-deserving husband, Muttienus Sentianus, also called Veterius, who lived 39 years and with whom she lived 13 years.
EDCS-08201570.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Iunia Ianuaria to her husband, Muttienus Sentianus (also known as Veterius), who died at 39 after 13 years of marriage.

EDCS-08201571.translation: To the departed spirits. Publius Luccius Felix made this for his dearest, well-deserving wife, Muttiena Gemellina, who lived 17 years.
EDCS-08201571.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Publius Luccius Felix to his wife, Muttiena Gemellina, who died at 17.

EDCS-08201572.translation: To the departed spirits. Naevia Allia lived 25 years and 4 months. Allia Herois, her unhappy mother, made this for her most dutiful daughter.
EDCS-08201572.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Allia Herois to her daughter, Naevia Allia, who died at 25 years and 4 months.

EDCS-08201573.translation: For Commis, his well-deserving tent-mate, who lived 45 years.
EDCS-08201573.summary: A funerary monument for Commis, a tent-mate, who died at the age of 45.

EDCS-08201574.translation: Marcus Arrius Anicetus for his well-deserving wife, Pasidia Restituta.
EDCS-08201574.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Marcus Arrius Anicetus to his wife, Pasidia Restituta.

EDCS-08201576.translation: Petronia Secundilla, his daughter, made this for Gaius Petronius Phoebus.
EDCS-08201576.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Petronia Secundilla to her father, Gaius Petronius Phoebus.

EDCS-08201577.translation: Pilia Apicula, freedwoman of a woman, [made this for herself and] for her son Publius Pilis Felix, freedman of Publius, [and for] Publius Pilis Anoptus, freedman of Publius, [and for] Quintus Pomponius Ferracus, son of Titus. Eros [built a tomb] in the front, 12 feet [deep], [and lives] in the back, 14 feet.
EDCS-08201577.summary: A funerary monument with property dimensions, set up by Pilia Apicula for herself, her son, and others, likely related to burial plots.

EDCS-08201578.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a freedwoman named Selenis.

EDCS-08201579.translation: Politice, freedwoman of a woman, for herself and for Lucius Fabius, freedman of Gaius, [and others].
EDCS-08201579.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Politice, a freedwoman, and likely mentions her husband or other family members.

EDCS-08201581.translation: To the departed spirits. Sextus Propi[---], grandfather [---] unhappy [---] lived [---] years.
EDCS-08201581.summary: This is a funerary epitaph with significant lacunae, likely for a Sextus Propius, mentioning his age at death.

EDCS-08201583.translation: Quintus Raecius, son of [---], of the Sergia tribe.
EDCS-08201583.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that names Quintus Raecius and his tribal affiliation.

EDCS-08201584.translation: To the departed spirits. Rode/nus Felicissimus made [this] for himself, well-deserving.
EDCS-08201584.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Rodenus Felicissimus, who dedicated it for himself.

EDCS-08201585.translation: [---]ius Rufus, son of Gaius, of the Claudia tribe, made [this] for himself and his family while alive, for Tertia, his mother, for his son Lucius Rufus, for his freedwoman Caletyce, granddaughter, and for his wife.
EDCS-08201585.summary: This is a funerary monument (sepulchre) erected by a certain Rufus for himself and his family, including his mother, son, freedwoman granddaughter, and wife.

EDCS-08201586.translation: Lucius Saenius Cerdo, freedman of Lucius, ordered [this] to be made for himself and for Lucius Saenius Alexander, patron. In front 12 feet, in the field 20 feet.
EDCS-08201586.summary: This is a funerary monument inscription that records the dimensions of the plot and names the dedicator and his patron.

EDCS-08201587.translation: To the departed spirits. Salutacita, daughter of [---]tri, for [---] [daughter?] living 4 years, may the earth lie lightly.
EDCS-08201587.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young girl named Salutacita, noting her age and including a wish for her burial.

EDCS-08201588.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a freedwoman named Marmoris.

EDCS-08201589.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Tiberio Statorio Gemino colono fundi Pacciani homini sanctissimo animae innocentissimae Numisia Augusti nostri serva coniugi cum quo vixit annos XIIII sine querella Capriolus filius naturalis patri karissimo bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-08201589.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Capriolus, natural son, made [this] for his dearest father, Tiberius Statorius Geminus, a most holy colonus of the Paccian estate, an innocent soul, and for his wife Numisia, servant of our Augustus, with whom he lived for 14 years without complaint; they made [this] for the well-deserving father.
EDCS-08201589.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a son for his father, Tiberius Statorius Geminus, describing him as a holy and innocent man and mentioning his wife, Numisia.

EDCS-08201591.translation: To Lucius Teius, son of Lucius, [---]. To Teia, daughter of Lucius, [---]. To Sperata. To Lucius Sertius [---]. Arventia, freedwoman of the woman [---], [---] in front 10 feet [---] and Teia, daughter of I[---].
EDCS-08201591.summary: This inscription appears to be a boundary marker or a funerary dedication with multiple individuals named, including members of the Teia and Sertius families, dating possibly to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201592.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Marcus Terentius Theodotianus made this for his dearest grandmother, Terentia Stibadis, while alive.
EDCS-08201592.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Marcus Terentius Theodotianus for his grandmother, Terentia Stibadis, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201593.translation: [---] ICLR [---]. Gaius Tiberius Pudens, son of Lucius, and Tiberia Tertulla, daughter of Lucius, and [---]ipta Hilaria, his wife, took care to have this made.
EDCS-08201593.summary: This funerary inscription records the names of those who arranged for the monument's construction, including Gaius Tiberius Pudens, Tiberia Tertulla, and Hilaria, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201594.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Tisianus Silvester, who lived [---]X years, for [his] spouse.
EDCS-08201594.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary dedication for Tisianus Silvester, mentioning his age and intended recipient (his spouse), probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201595.translation: Gaius Valerius, son of Gaius, [---] Lucius Valerius, son of Lucius. Iunia [---]tratea.
EDCS-08201595.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, possibly naming individuals from the Valerius family, with an incomplete mention of Iunia Tritratea, dating likely to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201596.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Flavia made this for her mother, Valeria Sperata, deserving well.
EDCS-08201596.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Flavia to her mother, Valeria Sperata, indicating filial piety and likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201597.translation: To Gaius Valvennius Apollonius, by his heirs according to the will.
EDCS-08201597.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Gaius Valvennius Apollonius, funded by his heirs as stipulated in his will, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201598.translation: [---]ODI[---]. Virginia Magna, freedwoman of Titus, ordered this to be made in her will for Vergin[ius ---].
EDCS-08201598.summary: This funerary inscription states that Virginia Magna, a freedwoman, instructed in her will that this monument be erected for Verginus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201600.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Lucius Vitorios Fortunatus, who lived 13 years, 9 months. His mother Vitoria Briseis and his stepfather Publius Tamullius Eros made this for themselves and him. In front 12 feet, in the field 17 feet.
EDCS-08201600.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Vitorios Fortunatus, detailing his age and listing his mother and stepfather as the dedicator and providing dimensions, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201601.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Vitoria Urbica. Vitorious Onesimus for his well-deserving wife. Lived 41 years, with her husband 30 years.
EDCS-08201601.summary: This funerary dedication by Vitorious Onesimus for his wife Vitoria Urbica provides her age at death and the duration of their marriage, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201602.summary: This is a funerary inscription identifying the deceased, Publius Volumnius, and stating he is buried here.

EDCS-08201605.translation: By the arbitration of Sextus Al[...]
EDCS-08201605.summary: This inscription fragment likely records a legal arbitration or decision, mentioning Sextus Al[...].

EDCS-08201608.translation: To the most fortunate [?], who lived 99 years, 11 months, 28 days, 11 hours; his heirs made this.
EDCS-08201608.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of the deceased and that it was erected by his heirs.

EDCS-08201623.summary: This is a funerary inscription fragment, indicating a monument was erected for a deserving person.

EDCS-08201639.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucceria Phoebianus made this for his most devoted brother, Caius Baebius Felix, an Augustalis.
EDCS-08201639.summary: This is a funerary inscription, dedicated by Lucceria Phoebianus to his brother, Caius Baebius Felix, an Augustalis.

EDCS-08201642.translation: To [...], the sister of Asuivia, freedwoman of Gaius, and of [...].
EDCS-08201642.summary: This is a funerary inscription fragment, indicating a monument was dedicated to a woman named Asuivia and her sister.

EDCS-08201644.translation: To the memory of Claudia Terentia Marciana.
EDCS-08201644.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a monument to Claudia Terentia Marciana.

EDCS-08201645.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Felicissima, his well-deserving wife, with whom he lived well, faithfully, and worked for 30 years, and who departed by fate, and for Obinius, whom I had now acquired, with whom he lived for 3 years, 7 months, [?]; and to Augurinus the younger, who lived 18 years, this [monument]...
EDCS-08201645.summary: This is a complex funerary inscription from a husband to his wife Felicissima and his son Augurinus the younger, detailing their lifespans and his relationship with them.

EDCS-08201646.translation: Sextus Sattius Scaevola, son of Gaius, is buried here.
EDCS-08201646.summary: This is a funerary inscription identifying the deceased, Sextus Sattius Scaevola, and stating he is buried here.

EDCS-08201648.translation: To the divine power of Hercules Acherontinus. Vitalis, slave of the illustrious man Claudius Severus, a regional supervisor, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-08201648.summary: This is a dedication to the deity Hercules Acherontinus, made by Vitalis, a slave and regional supervisor for Claudius Severus.

EDCS-08201649.translation: To Jupiter Dolichenus, most excellent. Lucius Mummius Niger, Quintus Valerius Vegetus Severinus, Caucidius Tertullus, having fulfilled their vow as consuls.
EDCS-08201649.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Dolichenus by Lucius Mummius Niger, Quintus Valerius Vegetus Severinus, and Caucidius Tertullus, who fulfilled their vow as consuls.

EDCS-08201652.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Marcus Aelius Caesonianus Dionysius, primus pilus, from Amastris, a Roman knight, aged 65, an incomparable patron. Caesonia Hyacinthus and Apulus, his freedmen, made this for him, a well-deserving man.
EDCS-08201652.summary: A tombstone for Marcus Aelius Caesonianus Dionysius, a former centurion and Roman knight, erected by his freedmen.

EDCS-08201654.summary: An epitaph for Gaius Avidius Vitalis Augustalis, likely a freedman of an imperial freedman.

EDCS-08201655.text_edition: \n Vivit Caius Egnatius Cai libertus Caius Egnatius Cai libertus Ianuarius situs\n
EDCS-08201655.translation: Caius Egnatius Ianuarius, freedman of Caius Egnatius Caius, lives on.
EDCS-08201655.summary: An unusual epitaph stating that Caius Egnatius Ianuarius, a freedman, is still alive.

EDCS-08201656.translation: His most unhappy parents, Anteros and Gallitana, made this for their daughter, the slave of Pierinus Gavius, who lived 24 years, as was fitting for daughters to do for their parents, for themselves and their daughter.
EDCS-08201656.summary: A tombstone for an unnamed female slave of Pierinus Gavius, erected by her unhappy parents, Anteros and Gallitana.

EDCS-08201657.translation: Publius Gavius Iucundus, freedman of Publius. Gavia Iucunda and Gavia Arescusa, his daughter. In front 12 feet, in the field 12 feet.
EDCS-08201657.summary: A funerary monument marking a plot of land 12x12 feet, belonging to Publius Gavius Iucundus and his daughter.

EDCS-08201658.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lucius Minucius Sabinus, alive for himself and Iunia Su[---].
EDCS-08201658.summary: A funerary inscription for Lucius Minucius Sabinus, who made it for himself and Iunia Su[---].

EDCS-08201659.translation: To the Divine Shades. His son made this for Lucius Novius Vitalio, a well-deserving man.
EDCS-08201659.summary: A tombstone for Lucius Novius Vitalio, erected by his son.

EDCS-08201660.translation: Potestas, the darling of Acesta, placed.
EDCS-08201660.summary: A dedication by Acesta to Potestas, likely a beloved pet.

EDCS-08201661.translation: To the Divine Shades. Titasus for Verecunda, his fellow slave, a well-deserving woman.
EDCS-08201661.summary: A tombstone for Verecunda, erected by Titasus, her fellow slave.

EDCS-08201664.translation: To Julia Mamaea Augusta, mother of the Emperor.
EDCS-08201664.summary: This is an honorary inscription dedicated to Julia Mamaea Augusta, mother of the emperor, likely erected by the imperial family or on behalf of the state.

EDCS-08201665.translation: Publius Paccius Laetus, son of Publius, of the Galeria tribe, second man (a city official), according to the will and by decree of the decurions, made this place.
EDCS-08201665.summary: This inscription records the provision of a public space, likely a tomb or a monument, made by Publius Paccius Laetus, a local magistrate, in accordance with his will and a decurional decree.

EDCS-08201666.translation: [Name] son of Quintus Pollio, a second time; [Name] son of [Name], Severus, second men (city officials); by decree of the Senate, they took care to have this made.
EDCS-08201666.summary: This inscription indicates that two individuals, identified as city officials and possibly related, were responsible for the construction of a public work, authorized by a senatorial decree.

EDCS-08201668.translation: To Publius Paculeius Rufus, son of Quintus, of the Romilia tribe, according to his will and by the arbitration of Gaius Clusenus, son of Gaius, of the Galeria tribe.
EDCS-08201668.summary: This inscription records a bequest, likely for a public work or monument, to Publius Paculeius Rufus, which was executed under the supervision of Gaius Clusenus, as stipulated in the will.

EDCS-08201676.translation: To Gaius Baebius Lupus, son of Gaius, of the Galeria tribe, fourth man (a city official), aedile, fourth man with judicial power, quaestor, quinquennial fourth man; the urban plebs, by means of a monetary contribution, the place was given by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-08201676.summary: This inscription honors Gaius Baebius Lupus, a prominent local official, and records that a public space was granted by the decurions through contributions from the urban plebs.

EDCS-08201677.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-08201677.translation: Marcus Mindius Maximus, son of Marcus, of the Galeria tribe, erected a statue of Mercury in honor of his quinquennality; on the occasion of its dedication, he gave to the people one denarius per person; the place was given by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-08201677.summary: This inscription records the erection of a statue of Mercury by Marcus Mindius Maximus, a local official, in honor of his quinquennial office, which included a donative to the populace and was approved by the decurions.

EDCS-08201678.translation: To Marcus Mindius Maximus, son of Marcus, of the Galeria tribe, fourth man, aedile, fourth man with judicial power, quaestor, quinquennial fourth man; [to] Mindius Proculus, son of Marcus, of the Galeria tribe; to Mindia [name], daughter of Marcus; to Marcus [Min]dius Livianus, son of [Name], of the Galeria tribe; the urban plebs, by a monetary contribution; on the occasion of whose dedication, he gave to the decurions and the people of both sexes one denarius; the place was given by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-08201678.summary: This inscription honors multiple members of the Mindius family, likely local officials, and records contributions from the urban plebs for a dedication ceremony that included a donative, all approved by the decurions.

EDCS-08300228.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aurelio Valenti veterano Iulia Optata coniugi bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08300228.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Iulia Optata made this for Marcus Aurelius Valentius, her deserving veteran husband.
EDCS-08300228.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a veteran named Marcus Aurelius Valentius, erected by his wife Iulia Optata.

EDCS-08300229.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus lio Dionysio ia Modesta coniugi pientissimo cum quo vixit annos XV fecit\n
EDCS-08300229.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Modesta made this for her most devoted husband Dionysius, with whom she lived for 15 years.
EDCS-08300229.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Dionysius, erected by his devoted wife Modesta, who states they were married for 15 years.

EDCS-08300230.text_edition: \n Vivo Marco Tadio Marci filio vivae Marciae |(mulieris) libertae Marcus Tadius Publi filius A fecit filio et\n
EDCS-08300230.translation: While alive, Marcus Tadius made this for his living son Marcus Tadius, son of Marcus, and for Marcia, a freedwoman, daughter of [Name]; Marcus Tadius, son of Publius, made this for his son and [---]
EDCS-08300230.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Marcus Tadius for his son Marcus Tadius and a freedwoman named Marcia, likely a funerary monument or a dedication of property.

EDCS-08400162.text_edition: \n Publius Arrius Publi filius Oufentina Rufus Magia Luci filia Rufa mater fecit de suo\n
EDCS-08400162.translation: Publius Arrius Rufus, son of Publius, of the Oufentina tribe, and Magia Rufa, daughter of Lucius, his mother, made this at their own expense.
EDCS-08400162.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up at his own expense by Publius Arrius Rufus, with his mother Magia Rufa also mentioned, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-08400163.text_edition: \n Aulus Baebius Auli libertus Anoptes Antonia |(mulieris) liberta Chreste Aulus Baebius Auli libertus Hilarus Antonia |(mulieris) liberta Calityche\n
EDCS-08400163.translation: Aulus Baebius Anoptes, freedman of Aulus, and Chreste, freedwoman of Antonia, [and] Aulus Baebius Hilarus, freedman of Aulus, [and] Calityche, freedwoman of Antonia.
EDCS-08400163.summary: This inscription lists four freedmen and freedwomen, likely related or associated with patrons named Aulus and Antonia, possibly from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08400167.text_edition: \n Publi Libuscidi Victorini abunculi sic Claudi Fortunati abunculi sic maioris Decimi Satreni Claudi Satreniani equitis Romani Eugeni\n
EDCS-08400167.translation: Of Decimus Satrenius Eugenius, Roman eques, of the family of Claudius Satrenianus, of the family of his greater uncle Claudius Fortunatus, of the family of his uncle Publius Libuscidius Victorinus.
EDCS-08400167.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Decimus Satrenius Eugenius, a Roman equestrian, and detailing his family connections, possibly dating to the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08400171.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Seppiae Amara<n>tini Seppius Ampliatus cosservae sic et Pactolus matri bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-08400171.translation: To the Divine Shades. Seppius Ampliatus and Pactolus, his co-servants, set this up for Seppia Amarantina, their well-deserving fellow servant.
EDCS-08400171.summary: This is a funerary dedication by two servants, Seppius Ampliatus and Pactolus, to their fellow servant Seppia Amarantina, suggesting a shared household or estate, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-08400172.translation: Avidia Cti to Iustus, her disciple, her husband.
EDCS-08400172.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Avidia Cti to her husband, Iustus, whom she also calls her disciple, potentially a scholarly or religious context, dating uncertain but likely Imperial period.

EDCS-08400174.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Constanti filio Flavio Valerio Constantino Pio Felici Invicto Augusto consuli III imperatori VIIII patri patriae proconsuli\n
EDCS-08400174.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, son of the deified Constantine, Consul for the 3rd time, Imperator for the 9th time, Father of the Fatherland, Proconsul.
EDCS-08400174.summary: This is a formal titulature of Emperor Constantine the Great, likely part of a public monument, dating to his reign (306-337 CE).

EDCS-08500148.text_edition: \n Lucius Trebius Luci libertus Dicinai sic locum sibi et sueis sic\n
EDCS-08500148.translation: Lucius Trebius Dicinai, freedman of Lucius, [dedicated] this place for himself and his family.
EDCS-08500148.summary: This is an dedication of a place by Lucius Trebius Dicinai, a freedman, for himself and his heirs, likely an urban or suburban plot, possibly from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-08500149.summary: This is a very brief inscription that likely served as a label or dedication for a person named Vicaria Eutacta, possibly funerary or ownership related.

EDCS-08500150.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gelasmo Sittiae servo vixit annos XXV menses III dies XXI ex collegio Herculis et Apollinis\n
EDCS-08500150.translation: To the Divine Shades. Gelasmus, servant of Sittia, lived 25 years, 3 months, and 21 days. From the collegium of Hercules and Apollo.
EDCS-08500150.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gelasmus, a servant of Sittia, who was a member of the collegium of Hercules and Apollo, providing precise age and collegial affiliation, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-08500151.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuniae Trophimae Caius Papirius Felix coniugi bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08500151.translation: To the Divine Shades. Caius Papirius Felix made this for his well-deserving wife, Junia Trophima.
EDCS-08500151.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Caius Papirius Felix to his wife Junia Trophima, highlighting her merit, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08500152.text_edition: \n stilia liberta Attica sibi et Lucio Caledio Licino viro et cursori et gemellis duobus vernis meis et Anniae Faustae arbitratu Heroidis libertae et Quinti Pollioni Fructi\n
EDCS-08500152.translation: Attica, Stilius' freedwoman, [erected this] for herself and for Lucius Caledius Licinus, my husband, courier, and for two twin boys, my slaves, and for Annia Faustae, under the arbitration of Herodis, freedwoman, and Quintus Pollio Fructus.
EDCS-08500152.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a freedwoman for herself and several individuals, possibly family members and clients, with a reference to arbitration.

EDCS-08500153.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Phileto Triphosa conservo et Philetus patri bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-08500153.translation: Philetus and Philetus made [this] for Philetus, a devoted slave, and for their well-deserving father.
EDCS-08500153.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by two individuals named Philetus for their father and a devoted slave.

EDCS-08500154.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Rosciae Cai filiae Iustinae Marcus Papius Marci filius\n
EDCS-08500154.translation: Marcus Papius, son of Marcus, [made this] for Roscia Iustina, daughter of Caius.
EDCS-08500154.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Marcus Papius for Roscia Iustina.

EDCS-08500155.text_edition: \n lus Silus fecit sibi de suo\n
EDCS-08500155.translation: Silus Ius made [this] for himself from his own resources.
EDCS-08500155.summary: This is a self-funded funerary dedication by Silus Ius.

EDCS-08500156.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ursae coniugi cum qua vixit nnos XIX Munatius Ianuarius cum qua sine ulla iniuria vixit pientissime bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08500156.translation: Munatius Ianuarius made [this] for Ursa, his wife, with whom he lived for 19 years, with whom he lived most devotedly and without any dispute, for her well-deserving.
EDCS-08500156.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Munatius Ianuarius for his wife Ursa, emphasizing their long and harmonious marriage.

EDCS-08500157.text_edition: \n Marco Anneio Marci filio Honorato Lucio Anneio Marci filio Catulo Marco Aneio sic Marci liberto Rufioni in fronte pedes XII in agro pedes X\n
EDCS-08500157.translation: For Marcus Anneius Honoratus, son of Marcus, for Lucius Anneius Catulus, son of Marcus, for Rufus, freedman of Marcus Anneius, 12 feet in front, 10 feet in the field.
EDCS-08500157.summary: This inscription records the dimensions of a plot of land allocated for the burial of three individuals with the same family name, possibly brothers and a freedman.

EDCS-08500158.text_edition: \n Manibus Pontiae Anniae vixit annos VII menses VI dies XVII Annius pater et Pontia Agrippina mater filiae pientissimae fecerunt\n
EDCS-08500158.translation: Annius the father and Pontia Agrippina the mother made [this] for their most devoted daughter, Pontia Annia, who lived 7 years, 6 months, and 17 days.
EDCS-08500158.summary: This is a funerary dedication by parents for their young daughter, Pontia Annia, specifying her age at death.

EDCS-08500159.text_edition: \n Terentia Patris liberta Terentio Clementi patrono Lucio Terentio Crescenti filio Caio Calidio Syngrapho contubernali in fronte pedes XII in agro pedes XII\n
EDCS-08500159.translation: Terentia, Terentius Clementi's freedwoman and contubernalis, [dedicated this] for Terentius Clementi, her patron, and for Lucius Terentius Crescenti, her son, and for Gaius Calidius Syngraphus, her fellow soldier; 12 feet in front, 12 feet in the field.
EDCS-08500159.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a freedwoman for her patron, son, and fellow soldier, also indicating plot dimensions.

EDCS-08500160.text_edition: \n Lucius Aemilius Speratus Apollinaris et Augustalis sibi et Suettiae Probae in fronte pedes XXX in agro pedes XXX\n
EDCS-08500160.translation: Lucius Aemilius Speratus Apollinaris and Augustalis [erected this] for themselves and for Suettia Proba; 30 feet in front, 30 feet in the field.
EDCS-08500160.summary: This is a funerary dedication by two individuals, Lucius Aemilius Speratus Apollinaris and Augustalis, for themselves and Suettia Proba, with specified plot dimensions.

EDCS-08500161.translation: Hyalissus, the Lucerian, beloved pet of Socconus Frentonis.
EDCS-08500161.summary: This is a dedication for a beloved pet dog named Hyalissus.

EDCS-08500162.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Pontia liberta Successa sive Tertia sibi et Marco Valerio Prisco Tito Satrio Longino contubernalibus posterisque suis in fronte pedes X in agro pedes X\n
EDCS-08500162.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Pontia Successa, freedwoman of (or possibly named) Tertia, for herself and for Marcus Valerius Priscus Titus Satrius Longinus, her fellow soldiers, and for their descendants. 10 feet in front, 10 feet in the field.
EDCS-08500162.summary: This funerary epitaph records a burial plot purchased by Pontia Successa for herself, her companion(s), and descendants, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500163.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Spediae Nice Marcus Titius Primitivos sic coniugi bene merenti fecit vixit annos XXX menses VI dies XX\n
EDCS-08500163.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Titius Primitivos made this for his deserving wife Spedia Nice, who lived 30 years, 6 months, and 20 days.
EDCS-08500163.summary: This funerary epitaph records a monument erected by Marcus Titius Primitivos for his deceased wife, Spedia Nice, including her age at death, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500164.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Sallustio Cai filio Ismariano aedili Caius Sallustius Ismarianus filio pientissimo vixit annos XXVI menses III dies XI\n
EDCS-08500164.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Gaius Sallustius Ismarianus, son of Gaius. Gaius Sallustius Ismarianus (his father) made this for his most devoted son, who lived 26 years, 3 months, and 11 days.
EDCS-08500164.summary: This funerary epitaph records a monument erected by a father for his deceased son, Gaius Sallustius Ismarianus, noting his age at death, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500165.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Lattio Prisco Augustali Luceriae Asbestus libertus patrono bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08500165.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Asbestus, freedman of Luceria, made this for his deserving patron Marcus Lattius Priscus, Augustalis.
EDCS-08500165.summary: This funerary epitaph records a monument dedicated by a freedman to his deceased patron, Marcus Lattius Priscus, who held a position as Augustalis, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500166.text_edition: \n Caius Octavius Cai libertus Gratus situs mater fecit\n
EDCS-08500166.translation: Caius Octavius Gratus, freedman of Gaius, (and) his mother made this.
EDCS-08500166.summary: This funerary epitaph records a monument erected by a freedman and his mother, likely for another family member, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500167.text_edition: \n Caeciliae Birronis libertae Pyrallidi Marcus Laelius Marci libertus Donatus et Caecilia Felicula et Caecilia Faustilla bene merenti fecerunt in fronte pedes XII in agro pedes XII\n
EDCS-08500167.translation: For Caecilia Pyrallis, freedwoman of Birro. Marcus Laelius Donatus, freedman of Marcus, and Caecilia Felicula, and Caecilia Faustilla made this for (her) deserving (person). 12 feet in front, 12 feet in the field.
EDCS-08500167.summary: This funerary epitaph records a monument erected by three individuals for Caecilia Pyrallis, a freedwoman, specifying plot dimensions, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500168.text_edition: \n Tito Flavio Primigenio vixit annos XIIII menses VIII Titus Flavius Salvius et Luccia Fortunata parentes fecerunt\n
EDCS-08500168.translation: For Titus Flavius Primigenius, who lived 14 years and 8 months. Titus Flavius Salvius and Luccia Fortunata, his parents, made this.
EDCS-08500168.summary: This funerary epitaph records a monument erected by parents for their son, Titus Flavius Primigenius, detailing his age at death, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500169.text_edition: \n Lucius Titius Luci libertus Adiutor Augustalis sibi et Lucio Titio Cai filio Sannioni patrono et Lucio Titio Secundo liberto meo Augustali\n
EDCS-08500169.translation: Lucius Titius Adiutor, freedman of Lucius, an Augustalis, for himself, and for Lucius Titius Sannio, son of Caius, his patron, and for Lucius Titius Secundus, my freedman Augustalis.
EDCS-08500169.summary: This funerary epitaph records a burial plot designated by Lucius Titius Adiutor for himself, his patron, and another freedman who was also an Augustalis, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500170.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Publius Velleius Donatus et sibi Pompeiae Ampliatae coniugi bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08500170.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Publius Velleius Donatus made this for himself and for Pompeia Ampliata, his deserving wife.
EDCS-08500170.summary: This funerary epitaph records a monument erected by Publius Velleius Donatus for himself and his wife, Pompeia Ampliata, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500171.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Villio Lascivo Villia Primigenia coniugi bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08500171.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Villia Primigenia made this for her deserving husband, Gaius Villius Lascivus.
EDCS-08500171.summary: This funerary epitaph records a monument erected by a wife for her deceased husband, Gaius Villius Lascivus, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500172.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Traebonia sic Dorcas Autonoeni vernae fecit quae vixit VIII annos XXV dies bene merenti\n
EDCS-08500172.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Traebonia Secunda, freedwoman of Autonoenus, made this for her slave Dorcas, who lived 8 years and 25 days, a deserving woman.
EDCS-08500172.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Traebonia Secunda, dedicated to her slave Dorcas, with details of the slave's lifespan.

EDCS-08500173.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sallustiae Hermionae Caius Sallustius Gennaeus filiae fecit vixit annos IIII dies VI\n
EDCS-08500173.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caius Sallustius Gennaeus made this for his daughter Sallustia Hermiona, who lived 4 years and 6 days.
EDCS-08500173.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life and death of Sallustia Hermiona, dedicated by her father Caius Sallustius Gennaeus.

EDCS-08500174.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Numisio Proculo Satria Maxima coniugi cum quo vixit annos XIIII bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08500174.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Satria Maxima made this for her husband Numisius Proculus, a deserving man, with whom she lived 14 years.
EDCS-08500174.summary: This funerary epitaph is dedicated by Satria Maxima to her deserving husband Numisius Proculus, noting their 14 years of marriage.

EDCS-08500175.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Chrysaspi Aci Priscini servo Chrysaspis filius bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08500175.translation: To the spirits of the departed. The son of Acius Priscinius made this for his deserving slave Chrysaspis.
EDCS-08500175.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication by the son of Acius Priscinius to his deserving slave Chrysaspis.

EDCS-08500176.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Naeviae Restitutae Muttiena Felicula amicae merenti posuit\n
EDCS-08500176.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Muttiena Felicula placed this for her deserving friend Naevia Restituta.
EDCS-08500176.summary: This funerary epitaph is a dedication by Muttiena Felicula to her deserving friend Naevia Restituta.

EDCS-08500178.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberio Claudio Severo vixit annum menses VII dies XXVI parentes fecerunt\n
EDCS-08500178.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Tiberius Claudius Severus lived 1 year, 7 months, and 26 days. His parents made this.
EDCS-08500178.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Tiberius Claudius Severus, dedicated by his parents.

EDCS-08500179.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Diodoro Diodorus saltuarius fecit filio et Eutychi et sibi di sic coniugi karissimae\n
EDCS-08500179.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Diodorus the forester made this for his son Diodorus, and for Eutyches, and for himself, and for his dearest wife.
EDCS-08500179.summary: This funerary epitaph was made by Diodorus the forester for his son Diodorus, Eutyches, and his wife, indicating multiple burials.

EDCS-08500180.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Ocratio Corintho vixit annos XXX dies XI Ocratia Silvana filio piissimo bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08500180.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Ocratia Silvana made this for her most dutiful son Lucius Ocratio Corinthus, who lived 30 years and 11 days, a deserving man.
EDCS-08500180.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life of Lucius Ocratio Corinthus, dedicated by his mother Ocratia Silvana.

EDCS-08500181.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Martinae quae vicsit sic annos VII menses VIIII dies XIIII Martialis filiae dulcissimae fecit\n
EDCS-08500181.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Martialis made this for her daughter Martina, who lived 7 years, 9 months, and 14 days, her sweetest daughter.
EDCS-08500181.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Martina, dedicated by her mother Martialis.

EDCS-08500182.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Felicitati quae vixit annos IIII menses IIII dies II Obsitia Leuca filiae suae dulcissimae fecit\n
EDCS-08500182.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Obsitia Leuca made this for her sweetest daughter Felicitas, who lived 4 years, 4 months, and 2 days.
EDCS-08500182.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life of Felicitas, dedicated by her mother Obsitia Leuca.

EDCS-08500183.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavius Civilis miles ex numero Cimbrorum qui vixit annos XX Flavius Copius fratri dulcissimo\n
EDCS-08500183.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Flavius Civilis, a soldier from the Cimbri, who lived 20 years. Flavius Copius for his most dear brother.
EDCS-08500183.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flavius Civilis, a soldier from the Cimbri, set up by his brother.

EDCS-08500184.text_edition: \n o Consio Quarto consulari correctori Apuliae et Calabriae cui praeter haec bona quae cum singulis earum regionum civitatibus sunt communia quibus aut se veri \n
EDCS-08500184.translation: To Consius Quartus, former consul, governor of Apulia and Calabria, who in addition to these good things which are common with each of the regions' communities, by which either he himself...
EDCS-08500184.summary: This inscription honors Consius Quartus, a former consul and governor of Apulia and Calabria.

EDCS-08500185.text_edition: \n Marcus Aemilius Restitutus Atrellae bene merenti et sibi \n
EDCS-08500185.translation: Marcus Aemilius Restitutus for Atrella, who deserved well, and for himself.
EDCS-08500185.summary: This is a dedication by Marcus Aemilius Restitutus for Atrella and himself.

EDCS-08500186.translation: Cale, a freedwoman of [---], is buried here.
EDCS-08500186.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Cale, a freedwoman.

EDCS-08500187.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Publius Herrius Ursulus.
EDCS-08500187.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Publius Herrius Ursulus.

EDCS-08500189.text_edition: \n Statilio Sabiniano qui vixit annos LXXV filius bene merenti\n
EDCS-08500189.translation: For Statius Sabinianus, who lived 75 years. From his son, who deserved well.
EDCS-08500189.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a son for his father, Statius Sabinianus.

EDCS-08500190.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Seppio o dulcissimo qui vixit annis IIII mensibus V Seppius Proculus patronus bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-08500190.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Seppius, most beloved, who lived 4 years and 5 months. Seppius Proculus, patron, made this for him, who deserved well.
EDCS-08500190.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young child named Seppius, set up by his patron Seppius Proculus.

EDCS-08500191.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Alexandro qui vixit annum I menses V\n
EDCS-08500191.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Alexander, who lived 1 year and 5 months.
EDCS-08500191.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a child named Alexander.

EDCS-08500192.text_edition: \n Allio liberto Eutraphilo Allia Cypare sibi et contubernali bene merenti\n
EDCS-08500192.translation: Allia Cypare for her freedman Eutraphilus and for herself and her partner, who deserved well.
EDCS-08500192.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Allia Cypare for her freedman Eutraphilus, herself, and her partner.

EDCS-08500193.text_edition: \n teus Numeri filius teus Numeri filii filius I eus Numeri filii filius Claudia aeus Numeri filii filius Claudia us Numeri libertus Aristo manii filia Quarta Auli liberta Erotis \n
EDCS-08500193.translation: [---]teus, son of Numerius; [---]teus, son of the son of Numerius; [---]eus, son of the son of Numerius; Claudia [---]eus, son of Numerius; Claudia [---]us, freedman of Numerius, Aristo; daughter of Manius, Quarta; freedwoman of Aulus, Erotis.
EDCS-08500193.summary: This inscription appears to be a complex family record or dedication, possibly listing multiple individuals and their familial relationships or patrons.

EDCS-08500194.translation: Maxima, daughter of Marcus, made this.
EDCS-08500194.summary: This is a funerary epitaph made by Maxima for herself or a relative, with her patronymic indicating her father was Marcus.

EDCS-08500195.text_edition: \n IOCEIS Chio filio qui vixit annos XVII mala Persephone at omnia morte Chio dura gravis ia iam vitae cari orae certae iam iuv ia funestas mater\n
EDCS-08500195.translation: Alas, Persephone! The harsh and cruel death of my son Chio, who lived seventeen years, has taken him from life; cruel mother, I am now certain of life's grim fate.
EDCS-08500195.summary: This is a deeply emotional epitaph from a mother mourning her son Chio, who died at the age of seventeen.

EDCS-08500197.text_edition: \n Marco Egnatio Quinti rtioni Marco Egnatio Cai genti Vettiae |(mulieris) libertae Perusae\n
EDCS-08500197.translation: To Marcus Egnatius, son of Quintus, and to Marcus Egnatius, son of Gaius, of the family of Vettia. To Perusa, freedwoman of the man [Marcus Egnatius].
EDCS-08500197.summary: This inscription records the dedication to two individuals named Marcus Egnatius, likely father and son, and a freedwoman named Perusa.

EDCS-08500198.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luci Lucili Martialis vixit annos XXIII dies XXI Ca Quinta mater miserrima fecit\n
EDCS-08500198.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Lucilius Martialis lived twenty-three years and twenty-one days. His most wretched mother, Quinta, made this.
EDCS-08500198.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Lucilius Martialis, who died at the age of 23 years and 21 days, erected by his grieving mother Quinta.

EDCS-08500199.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Petilius Sedatus sibi et Veraniae Iustae cum qua vixi annos XXXVII\n
EDCS-08500199.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Petilius Sedatus [dedicated this] for himself and for Verania Iusta, with whom I lived for thirty-seven years.
EDCS-08500199.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Petilius Sedatus, dedicated for himself and his wife Verania Iusta, with whom he lived for 37 years.

EDCS-08500200.text_edition: \n Reduci filio dulcissimo qui vixit annos X menses X dies XXIIII Spes mater bene merenti\n
EDCS-08500200.translation: To my dearest son, who lived ten years, ten months, and twenty-four days. Spes, his mother, [dedicated this] to the well-deserving.
EDCS-08500200.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a beloved son who died at the age of 10 years, 10 months, and 24 days, erected by his mother Spes.

EDCS-08500201.text_edition: \n Caio Stlaccio Cai liberto Primogeni sic et Fabiae Tertullae et Secundo <a>mi<c=T>o et Sperato colliberto in agro pedes X in frunte sic pedes X \n
EDCS-08500201.translation: To Gaius Stlacius, freedman of Gaius, Primogenus, and also to Fabia Tertulla and Secundus, freedman, and Speratus, fellow freedman. Ten feet in the field, ten feet in the front.
EDCS-08500201.summary: This inscription likely marks a burial plot or property boundary, mentioning Gaius Stlacius, his freedmen, and others associated with him.

EDCS-08500204.translation: Secunda made this for her deserving freedman.
EDCS-08500204.summary: This is a funerary epitaph erected by Secunda for her deserving freedman.

EDCS-08500206.text_edition: \n Quintus Camurius |(mulieris) libertus Ledus vixit annos L hic situs\n
EDCS-08500206.translation: Quintus Camurius Ledus, freedman of [a woman named], lived fifty years. He is buried here.
EDCS-08500206.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus Camurius Ledus, a freedman, who died at the age of fifty.

EDCS-08500207.text_edition: \n Soterichus Iuvenalis libertus vixit annos XXII hic est situs\n
EDCS-08500207.translation: Soterichus Iuvenalis, freedman, lived twenty-two years. He is buried here.
EDCS-08500207.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Soterichus Iuvenalis, a freedman, who died at the age of twenty-two.

EDCS-08500208.text_edition: \n Antonia Titi liberta Eucumene hic sita\n
EDCS-08500208.translation: Eucumene, freedwoman of Antonia Titus, lies here.
EDCS-08500208.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Eucumene, a freedwoman, stating her burial place.

EDCS-08500209.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maxentius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis consul II pater patriae proconsul\n
EDCS-08500209.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maxentius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul for the second time, father of the country, proconsul.
EDCS-08500209.summary: This is a titulature inscription listing the full official titles of Emperor Maxentius, likely from a public monument during his reign (early 4th century CE).

EDCS-08500210.translation: For Marcus Gavius Maximus, prefect of the praetorian guard.
EDCS-08500210.summary: This is an inscription honoring Marcus Gavius Maximus, who held the high office of praetorian prefect, likely from a public building or dedication.

EDCS-08500211.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Felicissima serva Tutili Acronis vixit annum I menses II parentes filiae karissimae posue<r=I>(unt) \n
EDCS-08500211.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Felicitassima, slave of Tutilius Acro, lived 1 year and 2 months. Her parents set (this up) for their dearest daughter.
EDCS-08500211.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Felicitassima, a child slave, erected by her parents, dating to her short life (likely Imperial period).

EDCS-08500212.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Volumnia Euacri serva vixit annos LV Silvano coniuci sic bene merenti\n
EDCS-08500212.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Volumnia Euacris, slave, who lived 55 years, (erected by) Silvanus, her husband, who deserved so well.
EDCS-08500212.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Volumnia Euacris, a slave, erected by her husband, Silvanus, detailing her age and their relationship (likely Imperial period).

EDCS-08500214.translation: For Gnaeus Cornelius, son of Publius.
EDCS-08500214.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication or funerary marker, identifying Gnaeus Cornelius by his filiation (name and father's name).

EDCS-08500215.translation: Publius Vinidius Zosimus, freedman of a woman.
EDCS-08500215.summary: This is a funerary inscription or marker identifying Publius Vinidius Zosimus as a freedman of an unnamed woman.

EDCS-08500217.text_edition: \n Pardalis Ancitii serva vixit annos VI hic iacet sepulta\n
EDCS-08500217.translation: Pardalis, slave of Ancitia, lived 6 years, lies buried here.
EDCS-08500217.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Pardalis, a child slave, stating her age and burial location.

EDCS-08500218.text_edition: \n Diis Manibus Tito Flavio Iucundo qui vixit annis XXX fecit Flavia Elpis coniuci sic bene merenti item collecius sic\n
EDCS-08500218.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Titus Flavius Iucundus, who lived 30 years. Flavia Elpis made (this) for her husband, who deserved so well; likewise, the association (made this for him).
EDCS-08500218.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Flavius Iucundus, erected by his wife Flavia Elpis and his association, indicating his age and relationships.

EDCS-08500219.translation: Olympinus, *duovir*, made (this) with his own money.
EDCS-08500219.summary: This is an inscription recording a public work or dedication made by Olympinus, who held the office of *duovir* (a local magistrate), funded personally.

EDCS-08600124.text_edition: \n Numerius Granius Numeri Philocalus Augustalis et Egnatia Quinti liberta Potestas vivi sibi fecerunt et Quinto Egnatio Publi filio Marso hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-08600124.translation: Numerius Granius Philocalus, freedman of Numerius, and his wife Egnatia Potestas, freedwoman of Quinto, made this monument for themselves while alive, and for their son Quintus Egnatius Marsus, son of Publius; it will not pass to their heir.
EDCS-08600124.summary: This is a funerary monument established by a couple for themselves and their son, likely dating to the early Imperial period.

EDCS-08600126.text_edition: \n ------ s]acrum Marcus Stlaccius Publius Naelius magistri\n
EDCS-08600126.translation: Sacred; Marcus Stlaccius Publius and Naelius were magistrates.
EDCS-08600126.summary: This inscription records two magistrates, Marcus Stlaccius Publius and Naelius, possibly from the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-08600128.translation: Epicarpia made this for her fellow slave Epitynchanus.
EDCS-08600128.summary: This is a dedication made by a woman named Epicarpia for her fellow slave, Epitynchanus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-08600130.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Hadrian, likely dating to his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-08600131.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus ntropi Vibius Vitalis bene merenti filio\n
EDCS-08600131.translation: To the Divine Shades; Vibius Vitalis for his son Ntropi, who deserved well.
EDCS-08600131.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Vibius Vitalis to his son Ntropi, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-08600133.text_edition: \n V militi cohortis IIII praetoriae equiti Aurelia Communis coniugi inconparabili cum quo vixit annos X menses III bene merenti procurante Primitivo contubernale et corniculario\n
EDCS-08600133.translation: To Aurelia Communis, his incomparable wife, for whom he lived 10 years and 3 months, who deserved well; Primitivus, his tent-mate and cornicen, acting as administrator.
EDCS-08600133.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a soldier of the Praetorian Guard to his wife, likely dating to the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-08900198.text_edition: \n Publius Lucius Postumii Luci filii Galeria Albinus et Niger Marsuam de sua pecunia fecerunt\n
EDCS-08900198.translation: Publius Lucius Albinus and Niger, sons of Lucius, of the Galeria tribe, made this from their own money.
EDCS-08900198.summary: This is a dedication of a public work or monument made by two individuals, likely from the Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-08900199.text_edition: \n Caio Rullio Maximo IIviro quinquennali Rullii Maximus et Priscus et Maximilla fili optimo patri fecerunt\n
EDCS-08900199.translation: For their most excellent father; Rullii Maximus, Priscus, and Maximilla, children, made this for Gaius Rullius Maximus, husband of the college of the IIviri, quinquennalis.
EDCS-08900199.summary: This is a funerary dedication by children to their father, who held local magistracies, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-08900200.text_edition: \n rius Nicephorus Augustalis sibi et ae Primae inae et Libadi libert\n
EDCS-08900200.translation: Nicephorus, freedman of [---]rius, Augustalis, for himself and for [---]ima Prima and Libada, freedman...
EDCS-08900200.summary: This inscription records a dedication for the dedicator and other individuals, including freedpersons, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-08900202.text_edition: \n Myrocl Luci libert-- s Vibinae liberta\n
EDCS-08900202.translation: Myrocl, freedman of Lucius --s, freedwoman of Vibius.
EDCS-08900202.summary: This inscription names two individuals, a freedman and a freedwoman, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-08900203.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records the name of Publius Licinius, possibly a magistrate or prominent citizen, with extensive restorations needed.

EDCS-08900204.text_edition: \n cum filis Provincia et Castina coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-08900204.translation: To Provincia and Castina, his wife, who deserved well.
EDCS-08900204.summary: This inscription is a dedication to a woman named Castina, wife of the dedicator, who is also referred to by the personified province Provincia.

EDCS-08900205.text_edition: \n agi filio Galeria patri Pollae matri tiae uxori\n
EDCS-08900205.translation: To Titius, son of Agius, for his father Galarius and his mother Polla, and his wife Tiatia.
EDCS-08900205.summary: This inscription is a dedication honoring multiple family members: father Galarius, mother Polla, and wife Tiatia, by Titius, son of Agius.

EDCS-08900207.text_edition: \n Marco Clodio Marci filio Oufentina Gallo Tettidiano IIIIviro iure dicundo aedili IIII pontifici Arrenae Luci filiae Maximae\n
EDCS-08900207.translation: To Marcus Clodius Gallo, son of Marcus, of the tribe Oufentina, from Tettidius, former IIvir iure dicundo, former aedile, former pontifex, and to Arrena Maxima, daughter of Lucius.
EDCS-08900207.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Marcus Clodius Gallo, a former magistrate and priest, and Arrena Maxima.

EDCS-08900208.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for Pomponia Primigenia.

EDCS-08900210.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Successus Laenillae servus vixit annos V menses VII dies XVI Soter et Dios filio pio posuerunt\n
EDCS-08900210.translation: To the Divine Shades. Successus, slave of Laenilla, lived 5 years, 7 months, and 16 days. Soter and Dios set this up for their pious son.
EDCS-08900210.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Successus, a slave, and is dedicated by his parents, Soter and Dios.

EDCS-08900211.summary: This inscription identifies ia as a freedwoman from Brundisium, with missing information about her former owner.

EDCS-08900212.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Furma sic Gerellani Modesti serva vixit annis XXII Caius Geminius Silbanus sic coniugi merenti posuit hic sita\n
EDCS-08900212.translation: To the Divine Shades. Furma, slave of Gerellanus Modestus, lived 22 years. Caius Geminius Silbanus set this up for his deserving wife. Buried here.
EDCS-08900212.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Furma, a slave, and is dedicated by Caius Geminius Silbanus for his wife, likely also named Furma.

EDCS-08900213.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Fortunata Tutoriae serva vixit annis X hic sita est Fortunatus coniugi bene merenti et sibi\n
EDCS-08900213.translation: To the Divine Shades. Fortunata, slave of Tutoria, lived 10 years. Buried here. Fortunatus set this up for his deserving wife and for himself.
EDCS-08900213.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Fortunata, a slave, and is dedicated by Fortunatus for his wife (possibly Fortunata) and himself.

EDCS-08900214.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Decimus C Proculus Hermaidi coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-08900214.translation: To the Divine Shades. Decimus C. Proculus set this up for his deserving wife, Hermaida.
EDCS-08900214.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication by Decimus C. Proculus for his deserving wife, Hermaida.

EDCS-08900215.text_edition: \n S Sisennae vixit nnos hic situs LXX sic\n
EDCS-08900215.translation: To Sisenna, lived 70 years, is buried here.
EDCS-08900215.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age and burial of Sisenna.

EDCS-08900217.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Agathangelus vixit annos LXV Carice coniugi piissimo hic situs est\n
EDCS-08900217.translation: To the Divine Manes. Agathangelus lived 65 years. Carice made this for his most devoted wife, she is buried here.
EDCS-08900217.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Agathangelus and was made by his wife Carice.

EDCS-08900219.text_edition: \n Diis Manibus Primitiva vixit annos XXXV coniux bene merenti fecit hic sita est\n
EDCS-08900219.translation: To the Divine Manes. Primitiva lived 35 years. Her husband made this for his well-deserving wife, she is buried here.
EDCS-08900219.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Primitiva and was made by her husband.

EDCS-08900221.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aprilis Appaliorum vixit annos XXXV Aurelius Primus coniugi vene sic merenti hic sita\n
EDCS-08900221.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aprilis, son of Appalius, lived 35 years. Aurelius Primus made this for his most deserving wife, she is buried here.
EDCS-08900221.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Aprilis and was made by her husband Aurelius Primus.

EDCS-08900223.summary: This funerary epitaph records the very young age of Cupitus.

EDCS-08900224.text_edition: \n vixit annos LX Exorien<s=T> coniugi bene merenti fecit hic sita est \n
EDCS-08900224.translation: He lived 60 years. Exoriens made this for his well-deserving wife, she is buried here.
EDCS-08900224.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of a woman and was made by her husband Exoriens.

EDCS-08900225.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Curo Petilli Severi servo vixit pius annos XVIIII Homulla coniugi\n
EDCS-08900225.translation: To the Divine Manes. Homulla made this for her devoted husband, the slave of Petillius Severus, who lived 17 years.
EDCS-08900225.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of a slave named Curo, dedicated by his wife Homulla.

EDCS-08900226.text_edition: \n Primus vixit annum I dies XXX\n
EDCS-08900226.summary: This funerary epitaph records the very young age of Primus.

EDCS-08900227.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Prima vicxit sic annos XVI Rufus bene meneti sic coniugi possuit\n
EDCS-08900227.translation: To the Divine Manes. Prima lived 16 years. Rufus made this for his well-deserving wife and placed it.
EDCS-08900227.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Prima and was placed by her husband Rufus.

EDCS-08900228.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Onesimus vixit annis XVIIII Balentina sic fiilio dulcissimo posuit Ingeninuia sic anni<s=A> XXX Ballentina sic materi filiae b<e=I>ne merenti posuit \n
EDCS-08900228.translation: To the Divine Manes. Balentina placed this for her most sweet son Onesimus, who lived 19 years, and for her daughter Ingenuinia, who lived 30 years, a well-deserving mother.
EDCS-08900228.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespans of a son, Onesimus, and a daughter, Ingenuinia, and was placed by their mother Balentina.

EDCS-08900229.text_edition: \n Birria Cognita Publi Birri Galli nutrix vixit annos LX hic sita\n
EDCS-08900229.translation: Birria Cognita, wet-nurse of Publius Birrius Gallus, lived 60 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-08900229.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life and burial of Birria Cognita, a wet-nurse.

EDCS-08900230.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Prigimenius sic Felicitati suae conservae fecit con sic qua vixit nnos XX\n
EDCS-08900230.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Prigimenius made this for his preserved slave and companion, with whom he lived 20 years.
EDCS-08900230.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication by Prigimenius to his deceased slave and companion, Felicitas.

EDCS-08900231.text_edition: \n Maecia liberta Prima hic sita est\n
EDCS-08900231.translation: Maecia Prima, the freedwoman, is buried here.
EDCS-08900231.summary: This funerary epitaph records the burial of Maecia Prima, a freedwoman.
